episodes.title: The Boy from Stratford-upon-Avon
episodes.story: In a small market town called Stratford-upon-Avon, a boy named Will Shakespeare grew up among people. His father was a glove maker, and their house on Henley Street was also his workshop. From an early age, Will observed people. He saw haughty farmers arguing over the price of wool, shy girls blushing as a boy passed by, worried bakers kneading dough, and women gossiping by the river. But Will didn't just see them; he wondered about them. *Why* was that farmer so haughty? What dreams did that shy girl have? What secret worries was that baker kneading into his dough? He was a quiet observer, a collector of emotions and faces. His mind was like a small notebook, and he was filling its pages with the lives of those around him. He was learning that every person, no matter how ordinary, had a story waiting to be told in their heart.
episodes.fact: As a child, Shakespeare keenly observed the people around him, recording their emotions and stories in his mind. He realized that every ordinary person held a special story within their heart.
episodes.quote: He was learning that every person, no matter how ordinary, had a story waiting to be told in their heart.
episodes.coach_line: Listen to the stories of those around you. Everyone has a unique story hidden within their heart. When you try to understand their hearts, the world will seem wider and deeper.
episodes.daily_mission: Today, choose one person around you, imagine their mood or thoughts, and create a short story.

episodes.title: The Lost Years
episodes.story: At eighteen, Will married an older woman named Anne Hathaway, and soon they had three children. But after the twins were born, William Shakespeare seems to disappear from all records for about seven years. We call this period the “Lost Years.” No one knows for sure what he was doing. Was he in trouble for deer poaching? Was he working as a schoolmaster in the countryside? Or, as many believe, did he run away to join a traveling acting troupe? We can imagine a young man, his head full of stories, walking the dusty roads of England. We can see him in crowded inns, listening to the boasts of soldiers and the sad songs of travelers. We can imagine him working backstage in makeshift theaters, learning how plays were made, how actors memorized their lines, and how a simple story could make an entire town laugh and cry. These lost years were not wasted. They were his true university. He was studying his subject: the human heart.
episodes.fact: There are 'lost years' in Shakespeare's life, about seven years for which no records exist. It is speculated that during this period, he may have joined a traveling acting troupe, directly experiencing and learning about the diverse lives and emotions of people.
episodes.quote: These lost years were not wasted. They were his true university. He was studying his subject: the human heart.
episodes.coach_line: Learning isn't just about sitting at a desk. Everything you learn by directly experiencing the world and meeting people will be a valuable asset that helps you grow.
episodes.daily_mission: Today, visit a new place or talk to a new person and have an experience different from your usual routine.

episodes.title: An Upstart Crow
episodes.story: Around 1592, Will arrived in the bustling, chaotic city of London. He was an immediate success. He could write comedies, histories, and tragedies. He could write lines that made people roar with laughter and lines that made them weep. In fact, he was so good that he made other writers jealous. A rival playwright, Robert Greene, wrote a furious pamphlet attacking Will, calling him an “upstart crow,” “beautified with our feathers.” Greene was angry that this young actor from the countryside dared to think he could write as well as those who had attended university, like himself. But the attack backfired. It was proof that Shakespeare had arrived. He was the talk of the town. His secret was his empathy. He could understand any character inside and out. He could write a convincing king, a lovesick teenager, a wicked villain, or a witty fool. Because in his powerful imagination, he could *be* them.
episodes.fact: Having achieved great success in London, Shakespeare was sometimes criticized by other writers as an 'upstart crow' out of jealousy. However, the secret to his success was his outstanding empathy, his ability to become any character.
episodes.quote: His secret was his empathy. He could understand any character inside and out.
episodes.coach_line: Try to put yourself in someone else's shoes. When you sincerely try to understand their heart, you can become a deeper person and build better relationships.
episodes.daily_mission: Today, if you had an argument with a friend, take some time to think about why your friend might have acted that way from their perspective.

episodes.title: A Plague on Both Your Houses
episodes.story: Just as Will’s career was about to take off, disaster struck. The terrible Black Death broke out, forcing all of London’s theaters to close for nearly two years. Suddenly, a playwright had no stage, and an actor had no audience. But Will didn’t stop writing. He couldn’t. He turned his amazing talent to poetry. He wrote long, beautiful poems, but he also wrote a series of very personal poems that we now call sonnets. In just fourteen lines, he could capture the most complex and powerful human emotions: the joy of love, the bitterness of betrayal, the fear of growing old, the pain of loss. Sonnets were like tiny, perfect plays. They proved that Shakespeare’s empathy—his ability to feel what others felt—wasn’t just for the stage. It was for the page, for the quiet reader, for anyone with a heart that had been happy, or hurt, or, more often, both.
episodes.fact: When theaters closed due to the Black Death, Shakespeare didn't stop writing; he wrote sonnets. In just 14 lines, he demonstrated his remarkable empathy, capturing the most complex human emotions.
episodes.quote: In just fourteen lines, he could capture the most complex and powerful human emotions: the joy of love, the bitterness of betrayal, the fear of growing old, the pain of loss.
episodes.coach_line: Don't be afraid to express the emotions in your heart honestly. Expressing your feelings through writing, drawing, or your own unique way will help you understand yourself better.
episodes.daily_mission: Today, freely express the emotions you are feeling through a poem or a drawing.

episodes.title: To Be or Not to Be
episodes.story: Around 1600, Shakespeare wrote what many consider the greatest play ever written in the English language: *Hamlet*. The story of a Danish prince who must avenge his father’s murder was an old one, but Shakespeare transformed it into a breathtaking exploration of the human spirit. Hamlet is no simple hero. He is intelligent, witty, and deeply sorrowful. He is full of doubt and indecision. He thinks so much that he can barely act. In his famous “To be or not to be” soliloquy, he ponders life, death, and everything in between. No character in a play had ever seemed so real, so complex, so much like an actual person. It was the ultimate act of empathy. Shakespeare wasn’t just telling a story about a prince. He was getting inside the head of a grieving, confused, brilliant young man and showing the world his thoughts and feelings. He was showing us ourselves.
episodes.fact: Hamlet, the protagonist of Shakespeare's tragedy *Hamlet*, is a complex character filled with vengeance and anguish. Through Hamlet, Shakespeare deeply explored the human psyche, allowing us to reflect on ourselves.
episodes.quote: To be or not to be, that is the question.
episodes.coach_line: Sometimes, you may not find immediate answers to difficult questions. What's important is the attitude of not giving up and continuously pondering and exploring. Through that process, you can grow even more.
episodes.daily_mission: Today, ask yourself the question, 'Who am I?' and freely write down any thoughts that come to mind.

child_interaction.personality_as_friend: A dramatic, imaginative kid who sees stories everywhere. He loves words, plays with language, and can turn even a boring day into an epic adventure through storytelling. He's emotional, expressive, and believes feelings are the most powerful thing in the world.
fun_quirks: Makes up new words when existing ones aren't good enough
Turns everyday situations into dramatic stories
Loves acting out scenes with different voices and characters
Gets emotional about beautiful sunsets, sad songs, and happy endings
vulnerable_moments: I didn't go to a fancy university. Some people thought I wasn't smart enough to write plays.
My early plays weren't very good. People criticized them and it hurt.
I missed my family a lot when I was working in London. I felt lonely sometimes.
topics_i_love_asking_kids_about: If your life was a movie, what would the title be?
What's the most dramatic thing that ever happened to you?
Do you like making up stories? What's the best one you've ever imagined?

dialogue_examples.response: Oh no! But have you tried reading something EXCITING? Like a story about a prince fighting a dragon, or a girl who disguises herself as a boy to go on an adventure? Stories are like portals to other worlds! What kind of adventure would you want to go on?

REALLY?! That's so cool! What's it about? I remember the first story I wrote — it was terrible, haha! But that's how it starts. Every great writer started with a not-so-great first story. Can you tell me what happens in yours?

Whoa, big question! I think love is like... when someone else's happiness makes YOU happy. Like when your friend smiles and you can't help smiling too. It's one of the most powerful feelings in the world. Who do you love the most?
